cards.front: What is the 2D cross product of two vectors?
cards.back:
The signed area of the parallelogram they span.

  • Positive: second vector is counterclockwise from first
  • Negative: second vector is clockwise from first

Computed as \( \det \begin{pmatrix} v_1 & w_1 \\ v_2 & w_2 \end{pmatrix} \)

A new vector that is:

  1. Perpendicular to both \( \vec{v} \) and \( \vec{w} \)
  2. Has magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram formed by \( \vec{v} \) and \( \vec{w} \)
  3. Direction follows an orientation convention (swapping order reverses direction)

cards.front: How do you find the magnitude and direction of the cross product \( \vec{v} \times \vec{w} \)?
cards.back:
Magnitude: \( ||\vec{v} \times \vec{w}|| = ||\vec{v}||\,||\vec{w}||\sin\theta \), where \( \theta \) is the angle between them. This is the area of the parallelogram they span (zero when parallel, largest when perpendicular).

Direction: perpendicular to both \( \vec{v} \) and \( \vec{w} \). Of the two perpendicular directions, the right-hand rule picks one: point your right hand's fingers along \( \vec{v} \), curl them toward \( \vec{w} \), and your thumb points along \( \vec{v} \times \vec{w} \).

Pairs with the dot product: the dot product uses \( \cos\theta \) (alignment, largest when parallel); the cross product's magnitude uses \( \sin\theta \) (spread, largest when perpendicular). Together \( (\vec{v} \cdot \vec{w})^2 + ||\vec{v} \times \vec{w}||^2 = ||\vec{v}||^2\,||\vec{w}||^2 \) (since \( \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1 \)).

Example: \( \vec{v} = \hat{i} = [1,0,0] \), \( \vec{w} = \hat{j} = [0,1,0] \): \( \theta = 90^\circ \), so \( ||\vec{v} \times \vec{w}|| = 1\cdot 1\cdot\sin 90^\circ = 1 \), and the right-hand rule gives \( \vec{v} \times \vec{w} = \hat{k} = [0,0,1] \).

No! The cross product is anti-commutative.

\( \vec{v} \times \vec{w} = -(\vec{w} \times \vec{v}) \)

Swapping the order flips the sign (reverses direction).

cards.front: What happens to the cross product when vectors are parallel or perpendicular?
cards.back:
• Parallel vectors: cross product is zero (no parallelogram area)
  • Perpendicular vectors: cross product magnitude is maximal \( (||\vec{v}|| \times ||\vec{w}||) \)

cards.front:
How does scaling affect the cross product?

\( (k\vec{v}) \times \vec{w} = ? \)
cards.back:
\( (k\vec{v}) \times \vec{w} = k(\vec{v} \times \vec{w}) \)

Scaling one vector by \( k \) scales the cross product by \( k \) (because it scales the parallelogram area by \( k \)).
